[SAM v1 — The BAM Format Specification] BGZF is block compression implemented on top of the standard gzip file
format.L.~Peter Deutsch,  GZIP file format specification
version~4.3, http://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc1952 RFC 1952.
The goal of BGZF is to provide good compression while allowing
efficient random access to the BAM file for indexed queries. The BGZF
format is `gunzip compatible', in the sense that a compliant gunzip
utility can decompress a BGZF compressed file.It is worth noting that there is a known bug in the Java 
  GZIPInputStream class that concatenated gzip archives cannot be
successfully decompressed by this class. BGZF files can be created and
manipulated using the built-in Java  util.zip package, but naive
use of  GZIPInputStream on a BGZF file will not work due to this
bug.

[SAM v1 — The BAM Format Specification] A BGZF file is a series of concatenated BGZF blocks, each no larger
than 64\,KiB both before and after compression. Each BGZF block
is itself a spec-compliant gzip archive which contains an ``extra field''
in the format described in RFC1952. The gzip file format allows the
inclusion of application-specific extra fields and these are ignored by
compliant decompression implementation. The gzip specification also
allows gzip files to be concatenated. The result of decompressing
concatenated gzip files is the concatenation of the uncompressed data.

[SAM v1 — The BAM Format Specification] Each BGZF block contains a standard gzip file header with the following
standard-compliant extensions:

[SAM v1 — The BAM Format Specification] enumerate
 The  F.EXTRA bit in the header is set to indicate that extra
  fields are present.
 The extra field used by BGZF uses the two subfield ID values 66 and 67 (ASCII `BC').
 The length of the BGZF extra field payload (field  LEN in the
  gzip specification) is 2 (two bytes of payload).
 The payload of the BGZF extra field is a 16-bit unsigned integer
  in little endian format. This integer gives the size of the containing
  BGZF block minus one.
enumerate

[SAM v1 — The BAM Format Specification] On disk, a complete BGZF file is a series of blocks as shown in the following
table.  (All integers are little endian as is required by RFC1952.)
table[ht]

[SAM v1 — The BAM Format Specification] tabular|l|l|l|l|l|r|
  1-6
  3|c| Field & 1c| Description & 1c| Type & 1c| Value \\1-6
  6|c|gray List of compression blocks (until the end of the file) \\2-6
  & 2l| ID1 & gzip IDentifier1 &  uint8\_t & 31 \\2-6
  & 2l| ID2 & gzip IDentifier2 &  uint8\_t & 139 \\2-6
  & 2l| CM & gzip Compression Method &  uint8\_t & 8 \\2-6
  & 2l| FLG & gzip FLaGs &  uint8\_t & 4 \\2-6
  & 2l| MTIME & gzip Modification TIME &  uint32\_t & \\2-6
  & 2l| XFL & gzip eXtra FLags &  uint8\_t & \\2-6
  & 2l| OS & gzip Operating System &  uint8\_t & \\2-6
  & 2l| XLEN & gzip eXtra LENgth &  uint16\_t & \\2-6
  & 5c|gray Extra subfield(s) (total size=XLEN) \\3-6
  & & 4c|gray Additional RFC1952 extra subfields if present \\3-6
  & &  SI1 & Subfield Identifier1 &  uint8\_t & 66 \\3-6
  & &  SI2 & Subfield Identifier2 &  uint8\_t & 67 \\3-6
  & &  SLEN & Subfield LENgth &  uint16\_t & 2 \\3-6
  & &  BSIZE & total Block SIZE minus 1 &  uint16\_t & \\3-6
  & & 4c|gray Additional RFC1952 extra subfields if present \\2-6
  & 2l| CDATA & Compressed DATA by  zlib::deflate() &  uint8\_t[ BSIZE-XLEN-19] & \\2-6
  & 2l| CRC32 & CRC-32 &  uint32\_t & \\2-6
  & 2l| ISIZE & Input SIZE (length of uncompressed data) &  uint32\_t & \\
  1-6
tabular
table

[SAM v1 — The BAM Format Specification] The random access method to be described next limits the uncompressed
contents of each BGZF block to a maximum of $2^16$~bytes of data.
Thus while  ISIZE is stored as a  uint32\_t as per the gzip format,
in BGZF it is limited to the range~$[0,65536]$.
 BSIZE can represent BGZF block sizes in the range~$[1,65536]$, though
typically  BSIZE will be rather less than~ ISIZE due to compression.

[SAM v1 — The BAM Format Specification] BGZF files support random access through the BAM file index. To achieve
this, the BAM file index uses virtual file offsets into the BGZF
file. Each virtual file offset is an unsigned 64-bit integer, defined as: 
  coffset6060 16124uoffset, where  coffset is an
unsigned byte offset into the BGZF file to the beginning of a BGZF
block, and  uoffset is an unsigned byte offset into the
uncompressed data stream represented by that BGZF block. Virtual file
offsets can be compared, but subtraction between virtual file offsets
and addition between a virtual offset and an integer are both
disallowed.

[SAM v1 — The BAM Format Specification] An end-of-file (EOF) trailer or marker block should be written at the end
of BGZF files, so that unintended file truncation can be easily detected.
The EOF marker block is a particular empty\ BGZF block
encoded with the default  zlib compression level settings,
and consists of the following 28~hexadecimal bytes:
center
1f 8b 08 04 00 00 00 00 00 ff 06 00 42 43 02 00 1b 00 03 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00
center

[SAM v1 — The BAM Format Specification] Empty in the sense of having been formed by compressing
a data block of length zero.

[SAM v1 — The BAM Format Specification] The presence of this EOF marker at the end of a BGZF file indicates that the
immediately following physical EOF is the end of the file as intended by the
program that wrote it.
Empty BGZF blocks are not otherwise special; in particular, the presence of
an EOF marker block does not by itself signal end of file.

[SAM v1 — The BAM Format Specification] An implementation that supports reopening a BAM file in append
mode could produce a file by writing headers and alignment records to it,
closing it (adding an EOF marker); then reopening it for append, writing
more alignment records, and closing it (adding an EOF marker).
The resulting BAM file would contain an embedded insignificant EOF~marker
block that should be effectively ignored when it is read.

[SAM v1 — The BAM Format Specification] The absence of this final EOF marker should trigger a warning or error
soon after opening a BGZF file where random access is available.\
When reading a BGZF file in sequential streaming fashion, ideally this
EOF check should be performed when the end of the stream is reached.
Checking that the final BGZF block in the file decompresses to empty
or checking that the last 28 bytes of the file are exactly the bytes
above are both sufficient tests; each is likely more convenient in
different circumstances.

[SAM v1 — The BAM Format Specification] It is useful to produce a diagnostic at the beginning of
reading a file, so that interactive users can abort lengthy analysis of
potentially-corrupted files.  Of course, this is only possible if the
stream in question supports random access.

[SAM v1 — The BAM Format Specification] The BAM format
BAM is compressed in the BGZF format. All multi-byte numbers in BAM are
little-endian, regardless of the machine endianness. The format is
formally described in the following table where values in brackets are
the default when the corresponding information is not available; an
underlined word in uppercase denotes a field in the SAM format.

[SAM v1 — The BAM Format Specification] tabular|l|l|l|p8.15cm|l|r|

  3|c| Field & 1c| Description & 1c| Type & 1c| Value \\1-6
  3|l| magic & BAM magic string &  char[4] &  BAM92 1\\1-6
  3|l| l\_text & Length of the header text, including any  NUL padding &  uint32\_t & $< 2^31$ \\1-6
  3|l| text & Plain header text in SAM; not necessarily  NUL-terminated &  char[ l\_text] & \\1-6

  & 2l| qual & Phred-scaled base qualities. See Section~sec:seq &  char[ l\_seq] & \\2-6
  & 5c|gray List of auxiliary data (until the end of the alignment block) \\3-6
  & &  tag & Two-character tag &  char[2] & \\3-6

footnote-1
As noted in Section~sec:alnrecord, reserved  FLAG bits
should be written as zero and ignored on reading by current software.
footnote
For backward compatibility, an absent  QNAME (represented as ` *' in SAM) is stored as a C string  "*92 0".

[SAM v1 — The BAM Format Specification] BIN field calculationsec:bin-field
 BIN is calculated using the  reg2bin() function in
Section~sec:code.
For mapped reads this uses $ POS-1$ (i.e.,~0-based left position) and the
alignment end point using the alignment length from the  CIGAR string.
For unmapped reads (e.g.,~paired-end reads where only one part is mapped, see
Section~sec:recommended-practice) and reads whose  CIGAR strings
consume no reference bases at all, the alignment is treated as
being of length one.
Note unmapped reads with  POS $0$ (which becomes $-1$ in BAM) therefore
use $ reg2bin(-1, 0)$ which is computed as $4680$.

[SAM v1 — The BAM Format Specification] N\_CIGAR\_OP fieldsec:ncigar
With 16 bits,  n\_cigar\_op can keep at most 65535 CIGAR
operations in BAM files. For an alignment with more CIGAR operations, BAM
stores the real  CIGAR, encoded the same way as the  cigar field in
BAM, in the  CG optional tag of type ` B,I', and sets  CIGAR to
`k Sm N' as a placeholder, where `k' equals  l\_seq, `m'
is the reference sequence length in the alignment, and ` S' and ` N'
are the soft-clipping and reference-clip CIGAR operators, respectively---i.e.,
in the binary form,  n\_cigar\_op=2 and  cigar= [k6060
41244,m6060 41243]. If tag  CG is present and
the first CIGAR operation clips the entire read, a BAM parsing library is
expected to update  n\_cigar\_op and  cigar with the real CIGAR
stored in the  CG tag and remove the now-redundant  CG tag.

[SAM v1 — The BAM Format Specification] SEQ and QUAL encodingsec:seq
Sequence is encoded in 4-bit values, with adjacent bases packed into the same byte starting with the highest 4 bits first.
When  l\_seq is odd the bottom 4 bits of the last byte are undefined, but we recommend writing these as zero.
The case-insensitive base codes ` =ACMGRSVTWYHKDBN' are mapped to $[0,15]$ respectively with all other characters mapping to ` N' (value 15).
[SAM v1 — The BAM Format Specification] Omitted sequence, represented in SAM as ` *', is represented by  l\_seq being 0 and  seq and  qual zero-length.

[SAM v1 — The BAM Format Specification] Base qualities are stored as bytes in the range $[0,93]$, without any +33 conversion to printable ASCII.
When base qualities are omitted but the sequence is not,  qual is filled with  0xFF bytes (to length  l\_seq).

[SAM v1 — The BAM Format Specification] Auxiliary data encodingsec:aux-type-codes
Optional alignment fields are stored immediately after each other immediately
following the  qual field, and are included in  block\_size.
Each field is represented as a two-character tag followed by a single type
character and then its value, whose length is determined by the field's type.

[SAM v1 — The BAM Format Specification] Single character ` A' fields have a total length of 4~bytes,
with the value represented as a single byte:
center
A1char
center

[SAM v1 — The BAM Format Specification] While all single (i.e., non-array) integer types are stored in SAM as ` i',
in BAM any of ` cCsSiI' may be used together with the correspondingly-sized
binary integer value, chosen according to the field value's magnitude.%
The signedness and size used for each integer value is an
implementation choice, but is typically the smallest that suffices.
Similarly floating point ` f' fields are represented as IEEE 754-2008
binary32 values.
Thus BAM numeric fields have a total length of~4, 5, or~7~bytes:
centerl@1inl
c1i8  (i.e.,  int8\_t)
    & i4int32\_t \\
C1u8  (i.e.,  uint8\_t)
    & I4uint32\_t \\
s2int16\_t
    & f4float \\
S2uint16\_t
    &
tabularcenter

[SAM v1 — The BAM Format Specification] String fields and hex-formatted byte arrays are represented as
 NUL-terminated text strings:%
The BAM representation of ` H' field values as textual
hexadecimal digits rather than binary data is for historical reasons.
Modern applications may prefer to use ` B,C' array fields rather
than ` H' fields.
centerl
Z1char1 NUL \\

[SAM v1 — The BGZF compression format] BGZF is block compression implemented on top of the standard gzip file
format.L.~Peter Deutsch,  GZIP file format specification
version~4.3, http://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc1952 RFC 1952.
The goal of BGZF is to provide good compression while allowing
efficient random access to the BAM file for indexed queries. The BGZF
format is `gunzip compatible', in the sense that a compliant gunzip
utility can decompress a BGZF compressed file.It is worth noting that there is a known bug in the Java 
  GZIPInputStream class that concatenated gzip archives cannot be
successfully decompressed by this class. BGZF files can be created and
manipulated using the built-in Java  util.zip package, but naive
use of  GZIPInputStream on a BGZF file will not work due to this
bug.

[SAM v1 — The BGZF compression format] A BGZF file is a series of concatenated BGZF blocks, each no larger
than 64\,KiB both before and after compression. Each BGZF block
is itself a spec-compliant gzip archive which contains an ``extra field''
in the format described in RFC1952. The gzip file format allows the
inclusion of application-specific extra fields and these are ignored by
compliant decompression implementation. The gzip specification also
allows gzip files to be concatenated. The result of decompressing
concatenated gzip files is the concatenation of the uncompressed data.

[SAM v1 — The BGZF compression format] Each BGZF block contains a standard gzip file header with the following
standard-compliant extensions:

[SAM v1 — The BGZF compression format] enumerate
 The  F.EXTRA bit in the header is set to indicate that extra
  fields are present.
 The extra field used by BGZF uses the two subfield ID values 66 and 67 (ASCII `BC').
 The length of the BGZF extra field payload (field  LEN in the
  gzip specification) is 2 (two bytes of payload).
 The payload of the BGZF extra field is a 16-bit unsigned integer
  in little endian format. This integer gives the size of the containing
  BGZF block minus one.

[SAM v1 — The BGZF compression format] The random access method to be described next limits the uncompressed
contents of each BGZF block to a maximum of $2^16$~bytes of data.
Thus while  ISIZE is stored as a  uint32\_t as per the gzip format,
in BGZF it is limited to the range~$[0,65536]$.
 BSIZE can represent BGZF block sizes in the range~$[1,65536]$, though
typically  BSIZE will be rather less than~ ISIZE due to compression.

[SAM v1 — The BGZF compression format] BGZF files support random access through the BAM file index. To achieve
this, the BAM file index uses virtual file offsets into the BGZF
file. Each virtual file offset is an unsigned 64-bit integer, defined as: 
  coffset6060 16124uoffset, where  coffset is an
unsigned byte offset into the BGZF file to the beginning of a BGZF
block, and  uoffset is an unsigned byte offset into the
uncompressed data stream represented by that BGZF block. Virtual file
offsets can be compared, but subtraction between virtual file offsets
and addition between a virtual offset and an integer are both
disallowed.

[SAM v1 — The BGZF compression format] An end-of-file (EOF) trailer or marker block should be written at the end
of BGZF files, so that unintended file truncation can be easily detected.
The EOF marker block is a particular empty\ BGZF block
encoded with the default  zlib compression level settings,
and consists of the following 28~hexadecimal bytes:
center
1f 8b 08 04 00 00 00 00 00 ff 06 00 42 43 02 00 1b 00 03 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00
center

[SAM v1 — The BGZF compression format] Empty in the sense of having been formed by compressing
a data block of length zero.

[SAM v1 — The BGZF compression format] The presence of this EOF marker at the end of a BGZF file indicates that the
immediately following physical EOF is the end of the file as intended by the
program that wrote it.
Empty BGZF blocks are not otherwise special; in particular, the presence of
an EOF marker block does not by itself signal end of file.

[SAM v1 — The BGZF compression format] An implementation that supports reopening a BAM file in append
mode could produce a file by writing headers and alignment records to it,
closing it (adding an EOF marker); then reopening it for append, writing
more alignment records, and closing it (adding an EOF marker).
The resulting BAM file would contain an embedded insignificant EOF~marker
block that should be effectively ignored when it is read.

[SAM v1 — The BGZF compression format] The absence of this final EOF marker should trigger a warning or error
soon after opening a BGZF file where random access is available.\
When reading a BGZF file in sequential streaming fashion, ideally this
EOF check should be performed when the end of the stream is reached.
Checking that the final BGZF block in the file decompresses to empty
or checking that the last 28 bytes of the file are exactly the bytes
above are both sufficient tests; each is likely more convenient in
different circumstances.

[SAM v1 — The BGZF compression format] It is useful to produce a diagnostic at the beginning of
reading a file, so that interactive users can abort lengthy analysis of
potentially-corrupted files.  Of course, this is only possible if the
stream in question supports random access.

uncompressed data stream represented by that BGZF block. Virtual file

[SAM v1 — The BAM format] Sequence is encoded in 4-bit values, with adjacent bases packed into the same byte starting with the highest 4 bits first.

The case-insensitive base codes `=ACMGRSVTWYHKDBN' are mapped to [0,15] respectively with all other characters mapping to `N' (value 15).

[SAM v1 — The BAM format] Base qualities are stored as bytes in the range [0,93], without any +33 conversion to printable ASCII.
When base qualities are omitted but the sequence is not, qual is filled with 0xFF bytes (to length l_seq).

[SAM v1 — Indexing BAM] Indexing aims to achieve fast retrieval of alignments overlapping a
specified region without going through the whole alignments. BAM must be
sorted by the reference ID and then the leftmost coordinate before
indexing.

[SAM v1 — Indexing BAM] This section describes the binning scheme underlying coordinate-sorted
BAM indices and its implementation in the long-established BAI format.
The CSI format documented elsewhere uses a similar binning scheme and
can also be used to index BAM.

[SAM v1 — Indexing BAM] See http://samtools.github.io/hts-specs/CSIv1.pdf
 CSIv1.pdf at https://github.com/samtools/hts-specs.
This is a separate specification because CSI is also used to index other
coordinate-sorted file formats in addition to BAM.

[SAM v1 — Indexing BAM] Algorithm
Basic binning index
The UCSC binning scheme was suggested by Richard Durbin and Lincoln Stein
and is explained in Kent  et al.\ In this scheme, each bin
represents a contiguous genomic region which is either fully contained
in or non-overlapping with another bin; each alignment is associated
with a bin which represents the smallest region containing the entire
alignment. The binning scheme is essentially a representation of
R-tree. A distinct bin uniquely corresponds to a distinct internal node
in a R-tree. Bin A is a child of Bin B if the region represented by A is
contained in B.

[SAM v1 — Indexing BAM] To find the alignments that overlap a specified region, we need to get
the bins that overlap the region, and then test each alignment in the
bins to check overlap. To quickly find alignments associated with a
specified bin, we can keep in the index the start file offsets of chunks
of alignments which all have the bin. As alignments are sorted by the
leftmost coordinates, alignments having the same bin tend to be
clustered together on the disk and therefore usually a bin is only
associated with a few chunks. Traversing all the alignments having the
same bin usually needs a few seek calls. Given the set of bins that
overlap the specified region, we can visit alignments in the order of
their leftmost coordinates and stop seeking the rest when an alignment
falls outside the required region. This strategy saves half of the seek
calls in average.

[SAM v1 — Indexing BAM] In the BAI format, each bin may span $2^29$, $2^26$, $2^23$, $2^20$,
$2^17$ or $2^14$ bp. Bin 0 spans a 512Mbp region, bins 1--8 span 64Mbp,
9--72 8Mbp, 73--584 1Mbp, 585--4680 128kbp, and bins 4681--37448 span 16kbp
regions.  This implies that this index format does not support reference
chromosome sequences longer than $2^29-1$.

[SAM v1 — Indexing BAM] The CSI format generalises the sizes of the bins, and supports reference
sequences of the same length as are supported by SAM and BAM.

[SAM v1 — Indexing BAM] Reducing small chunks
Around the boundary of two adjacent bins, we may see many small chunks
with some having a shorter bin while the rest having a larger bin. To
reduce the number of seek calls, we may join two chunks having the same
bin if they are close to each other. After this process, a joined chunk
will contain alignments with different bins. We need to keep in the
index the file offset of the end of each chunk to identify its
boundaries.

[SAM v1 — Indexing BAM] Combining with linear index
For an alignment starting beyond 64Mbp, we always need to seek to some
chunks in bin 0, which can be avoided by using a linear index. In the
linear index, for each tiling 16384bp window on the reference, we record
the smallest file offset of the alignments that overlap with the
window. Given a region [rbeg,\,rend), we only need to visit a chunk whose
end file offset is larger than the file offset of the 16kbp window
containing rbeg.

[SAM v1 — Indexing BAM] With both binning and linear indices, we can retrieve alignments in most
of regions with just one seek call.

[SAM v1 — Indexing BAM] A conceptual example
Suppose we have a genome shorter than 144kbp. We can design a binning
scheme which consists of three types of bins: bin 0 spans 0-144kbp, bin
1, 2 and 3 span 48kbp and bins from 4 to 12 span 16kbp each:

[SAM v1 — Indexing BAM] An alignment starting at 65kbp and ending at 67kbp would have a bin
number 8, which is the smallest bin containing the alignment. Similarly,
an alignment starting at 51kbp and ending at 70kbp would go to bin 2,
while an alignment between [40k,\,49k] to bin 0. Suppose we want to find
all the alignments overlapping region [65k,\,71k). We first calculate that
bin 0, 2 and 8 overlap with this region and then traverse the alignments
in these bins to find the required alignments. With a binning index
alone, we need to visit the alignment at [40k,\,49k] as it belongs to bin
0. But with a linear index, we know that such an alignment stops before
64kbp and cannot overlap the specified region. A seek call can thus be
saved.

[SAM v1 — Indexing BAM] The index file may optionally contain additional metadata providing a summary
of the number of mapped and placed unmapped read-segments per reference sequence,
and of any unplaced unmapped read-segments.By placed unmapped
read we mean a read that is unmapped according to its  FLAG but whose
 RNAME and~ POS fields are filled in, thus ``placing'' it on a
reference sequence (see Section~sec:recommended-practice).

number~37450 (which is beyond the normal range) and are laid out so as to be

[SAM v1 — Indexing BAM] C source code for computing bin number and overlapping binssec:code
The following functions compute bin numbers and overlaps for a BAI-style
binning scheme with $6$~levels and a minimum bin size of $2^14$~bp.
See the CSI specification for generalisations of these functions designed for
binning schemes with arbitrary depth and sizes.

[SAM v1 — Indexing BAM] When these functions are called with regions representing unplaced
unmapped reads, e.g., $ reg2bin(-1, 0)$, they involve operations
such as |(-1)>>14| which are undefined or implementation-defined in
some programming languages.
They must be implemented as if these operations use the common
two's-complement semantics: $ reg2bin(-1, 0) = 4680$ and
$ reg2bins(-1, 0, )$ returns $[\,0, 0, 8, 72, 584, 4680\,]$.

[SAM v1 — Indexing BAM] /* calculate the list of bins that may overlap with region [beg,end) (zero-based) */
#define MAX_BIN (((1<<18)-1)/7)
int reg2bins(int beg, int end, uint16_t list[MAX_BIN])

[SAM v1 — Algorithm] Basic binning index
The UCSC binning scheme was suggested by Richard Durbin and Lincoln Stein
and is explained in Kent  et al.\ In this scheme, each bin
represents a contiguous genomic region which is either fully contained
in or non-overlapping with another bin; each alignment is associated
with a bin which represents the smallest region containing the entire
alignment. The binning scheme is essentially a representation of
R-tree. A distinct bin uniquely corresponds to a distinct internal node
in a R-tree. Bin A is a child of Bin B if the region represented by A is
contained in B.

[SAM v1 — Algorithm] To find the alignments that overlap a specified region, we need to get
the bins that overlap the region, and then test each alignment in the
bins to check overlap. To quickly find alignments associated with a
specified bin, we can keep in the index the start file offsets of chunks
of alignments which all have the bin. As alignments are sorted by the
leftmost coordinates, alignments having the same bin tend to be
clustered together on the disk and therefore usually a bin is only
associated with a few chunks. Traversing all the alignments having the
same bin usually needs a few seek calls. Given the set of bins that
overlap the specified region, we can visit alignments in the order of
their leftmost coordinates and stop seeking the rest when an alignment
falls outside the required region. This strategy saves half of the seek
calls in average.

[SAM v1 — Algorithm] In the BAI format, each bin may span $2^29$, $2^26$, $2^23$, $2^20$,
$2^17$ or $2^14$ bp. Bin 0 spans a 512Mbp region, bins 1--8 span 64Mbp,
9--72 8Mbp, 73--584 1Mbp, 585--4680 128kbp, and bins 4681--37448 span 16kbp
regions.  This implies that this index format does not support reference
chromosome sequences longer than $2^29-1$.

[SAM v1 — Algorithm] The CSI format generalises the sizes of the bins, and supports reference
sequences of the same length as are supported by SAM and BAM.

[SAM v1 — Algorithm] Reducing small chunks
Around the boundary of two adjacent bins, we may see many small chunks
with some having a shorter bin while the rest having a larger bin. To
reduce the number of seek calls, we may join two chunks having the same
bin if they are close to each other. After this process, a joined chunk
will contain alignments with different bins. We need to keep in the
index the file offset of the end of each chunk to identify its
boundaries.

[SAM v1 — Algorithm] Combining with linear index
For an alignment starting beyond 64Mbp, we always need to seek to some
chunks in bin 0, which can be avoided by using a linear index. In the
linear index, for each tiling 16384bp window on the reference, we record
the smallest file offset of the alignments that overlap with the
window. Given a region [rbeg,\,rend), we only need to visit a chunk whose
end file offset is larger than the file offset of the 16kbp window
containing rbeg.

[SAM v1 — Algorithm] With both binning and linear indices, we can retrieve alignments in most
of regions with just one seek call.

[SAM v1 — Algorithm] A conceptual example
Suppose we have a genome shorter than 144kbp. We can design a binning
scheme which consists of three types of bins: bin 0 spans 0-144kbp, bin
1, 2 and 3 span 48kbp and bins from 4 to 12 span 16kbp each:

[SAM v1 — Algorithm] An alignment starting at 65kbp and ending at 67kbp would have a bin
number 8, which is the smallest bin containing the alignment. Similarly,
an alignment starting at 51kbp and ending at 70kbp would go to bin 2,
while an alignment between [40k,\,49k] to bin 0. Suppose we want to find
all the alignments overlapping region [65k,\,71k). We first calculate that
bin 0, 2 and 8 overlap with this region and then traverse the alignments
in these bins to find the required alignments. With a binning index
alone, we need to visit the alignment at [40k,\,49k] as it belongs to bin
0. But with a linear index, we know that such an alignment stops before
64kbp and cannot overlap the specified region. A seek call can thus be
saved.

text:
[SAM v1 — Parsing region notation] Parsing region notation such as name[:begin[-end]] (in which omission of the outer bracketed portion indicates a request for the entire reference sequence) would be simple if name could not itself contain `:' characters, but this is not the case.
(No such notation containing an optional `:' appears in the SAM format itself, but various tools use this notation as a convenient way for their users to specify regions of interest.)

[SAM v1 — Parsing region notation] The set of valid reference sequence names is usually already known when parsing this notation—for example, because the associated @SQ headers have already been encountered.
Tools can use this set to determine unambiguously which colons could delimit a known-valid reference sequence name.
[SAM v1 — Parsing region notation] In pseudocode form, a string str can be parsed as follows:
[SAM v1 — Parsing region notation] c̄onsider the rightmost `:' character, if any, of str
[SAM v1 — Parsing region notation] if str is of the form `prefix:NUM' ōr `prefix:NUM-NUM'
[SAM v1 — Parsing region notation] or generally `prefix:suffix' for some plausible interval suffix
[SAM v1 — Parsing region notation] īf both prefix and str are in the known set then  …error: ambiguous representation
[SAM v1 — Parsing region notation] else if prefix is in the known set then return (prefix, NUM…NUM)
[SAM v1 — Parsing region notation] else if str is in the known set then return (str, entire sequence)
[SAM v1 — Parsing region notation] else  …error: unknown reference sequence name
[SAM v1 — Parsing region notation] else    …either str does not contain a colon or the suffix is not plausibly numeric
[SAM v1 — Parsing region notation] if str is in the known set then return (str, entire sequence)
[SAM v1 — Parsing region notation] else  …error: unknown reference sequence name or invalid interval syntax

[SAM v1 — Parsing region notation] The check leading to “error: ambiguous representation” is important as it prevents confusing interpretations of actually ambiguous input.
Typically the set of valid reference sequence names will not contain names that are prefixes of other names in the set, so in practice this error will not usually be encountered in non-malicious data.

[SAM v1 — Parsing region notation] Either in addition to this algorithm or as an alternative to it, tools can use additional delimiter characters to make an unambiguously parsable notation.
We recommend a convention using curly brackets around the reference sequence name— name[:begin[-end]] —as being memorable, easily typed, unambiguous, and not expanded by most shells.
